story.heading: Supreme Court Rejects Trump’s Global Tariffs; Trump Jokes About Naming Ted Cruz to High Court
story.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act does not authorize President Donald Trump to impose global tariffs, a decision that critics and some Republicans say limits the administration’s trade tools and could affect political dynamics. Separately, during a visit to Texas, Trump floated nominating Sen. Ted Cruz to the Supreme Court and discussed possible action on Iran.

segments.heading: Supreme Court rules tariffs unlawful
segments.text: The Supreme Court held that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act does not authorize the president to impose the global tariffs that the administration had announced. Chief Justice John Roberts, writing for the majority, framed the decision as enforcing the court's limited constitutional role and rejecting an executive claim of broad tariff authority under that statute.

citations.quote: The Supreme Court on Friday dealt President Donald Trump perhaps the most significant rebuke of his second term. The justices, including two of the president9s appointees, struck down Trump9s signature economic policy: his global tariffs.
Fulfilling that role, we hold that (the International Emergency Economic Powers Act) does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.

segments.heading: Political and economic consequences
segments.text: Commentators and analysts cited by news outlets said the ruling removes a significant unilateral trade tool from the president and could ease some immediate economic uncertainty tied to tariff announcements. Coverage noted the decision may have political implications for the administration and the Republican Party, given economic indicators and public polling cited around the tariffs debate.

citations.quote: Since Trump announced the global tariffs back on April 2, his net economic approval rating in Nate Silver9s averages has gone from plus-6 to minus-12.
A CNN poll last month showed Americans disapproved of Trump on tariffs, 62%-37%.

segments.heading: Trump's reaction and broader context
segments.text: President Trump publicly criticized the decision and expressed frustration with the justices who ruled against him. Coverage described the contrast between Chief Justice Roberts' measured courtroom explanation and the president's response at the White House.

citations.quote: He called the justices who ruled against him a "disgrace to our nation."
"We claim no special competence in matters of economics or foreign affairs," Roberts elaborated ...

segments.heading: Trump floats Sen. Ted Cruz for Supreme Court and comments on Iran during Texas visit
segments.text: At an outdoor event in Corpus Christi, Texas, President Trump said he was "thinking about putting" Sen. Ted Cruz on the Supreme Court and joked that Cruz "will get 100% of the Democrat vote, 100% of the Republican vote," arguing senators would want to move him out of Congress. The president also discussed ongoing deliberations about possible action on Iran and said he had spoken with lawmakers, including Cruz, about the issue. The reporting also summarized Cruz's prior legal experience before the Court.

citations.quote: "I'm thinking about putting him in the Supreme Court. 63B... he's the only guy I know who'll get 100% of the Democrat vote, 100% of the Republican vote."

Cruz, who earned his law degree from Harvard Law School, argued eight cases before the Supreme Court as solicitor general of Texas before returning to private practice, where he argued his ninth before the highest court.
"We have a big decision to make. 63B... Not easy," Trump said, referring to possible action on Iran, and adding that "I spoke with Ted Cruz, John, all of them on the way here."

story.heading: Search Continues for Nancy Guthrie; Investigators Review Video, DNA and Neighborhood Leads
story.summary: Authorities continue to investigate the disappearance of 84-year-old Nancy Guthrie from her Tucson-area home on January 31, reviewing video footage and DNA evidence while canvassing the neighborhood and following tips. Officials say family members are not suspects and have not identified a leading motive.

segments.heading: Overview of disappearance
segments.text: Nancy Guthrie was last seen on January 31 at her home in the Catalina Foothills outside Tucson. Investigators say she disappeared from the residence without her phone and required medications, and they believe she was taken against her will. No leading theory for a motive has been announced.

citations.quote: Nancy Guthrie was last seen on January 31, before she was apparently kidnapped, disappearing from her secluded home in Arizonas Catalina Foothills without her phone  or critical medications.
Authorities say they believe Nancy Guthrie was removed from the home against her will. But investigators still have no leading theory about a motive

segments.heading: Evidence reviewed by investigators
segments.text: Law enforcement agencies, including the FBI and local investigators, are reviewing thousands of hours of video footage from the greater Tucson area and conducting laboratory analysis on DNA found in connection with the case. DNA from gloves located about two miles from Guthrie's home did not match profiles in CODIS nor the DNA found in the house. Investigators also reported that a person seen on doorbell camera carried an Ozark-brand backpack.

citations.quote: Teams of FBI and local law enforcement investigators are also reviewing "thousands of hours" of video obtained from the greater Tucson area
DNA on gloves found 2 miles from the home did not return any matches in the national database known as CODIS, and doesnt match DNA found the house, either, Nanos said.
Officials determine the man seen in doorbell video was carrying an Ozark backpack, the sheriff said

segments.heading: Neighborhood footage and local leads
segments.text: Local reporting highlights neighborhood-sourced video and witnesses as part of the investigation. Neighbors' Ring-camera footage has been reported to show vehicles on a possible route away from the scene, and at least one neighbor reported seeing a suspicious man near the area about two weeks before the disappearance.

citations.quote: NEIGHBOR EXCLUSIVE: Nancy Guthrie neighbors' Ring camera captures vehicles on possible route from crime scene
FOOTHILLS PHANTOM: Nancy Guthrie's neighbor saw suspicious man walking nearby 2 weeks before suspected abduction

segments.heading: Investigative coordination and cross-border inquiries
segments.text: Authorities have briefed U.S. Border Patrol and Mexican law enforcement as part of standard procedures given Tucson's proximity to the border. Mexican officials told reporters they had no indication Guthrie had been taken into Mexico and said the FBI informed them it had no leads suggesting she was in Mexico.

citations.quote: Mexican officials say there is currently no indication Guthrie was brought into Mexico.
The FBI informed us that they currently have no leads to suggest that this woman could be in Mexico, said Carlos Flores

segments.heading: Family status and public response
segments.text: Sheriff's officials have said Guthrie's adult children and their spouses are not suspects in the investigation. The case has prompted public attention, including video pleas from family members and activity by online tipsters, while investigators continue to ask anyone with relevant information to come forward.

citations.quote: Nancy Guthries adult children and their spouses are not suspects in her February 1 disappearance, the sheriff says, calling any suggestion otherwise cruel.
DIGITAL VIGILANTES: Nancy Guthrie disappearance fuels rise of mom detectives swapping tips and losing sleep

story.heading: U.S. Deploys F-22s to Israel as Tehran Faces Range of Retaliation Options
story.summary: Eleven U.S. F-22 Raptors have been positioned at Israel's Ovda Air Base amid a wider U.S. military buildup in the Middle East. U.S. officials and analysts say the deployment expands military options, while experts outline how Iran could retaliate using missiles, drones and proxies.

segments.heading: Deployment and regional buildup
segments.text: Eleven U.S. Air Force F-22 Raptors have arrived at Israel's Ovda Air Base, an action described in one report as the first operational deployment of American combat aircraft to Israel. The deployment occurs alongside a broader U.S. buildup of air and naval assets in the Middle East.

citations.quote: Eleven U.S. Air Force F-22 Raptor stealth fighters have arrived at Israel's Ovda Air Base in the country's south, Fox News reported, marking the first-ever operational deployment of American combat aircraft to Israel.
the US military is pressing ahead with a significant buildup of air and naval assets in the Middle East.

segments.heading: Capabilities the F-22 brings to the theater
segments.text: Retired U.S. officers and analysts cited the F-22's air-to-air and suppression capabilities, saying it is designed to operate in heavily defended airspace and could play a role in clearing the way for other strike aircraft or bombers.

citations.quote: "The F-22 is indeed the most air-to-air capable fighter in the world; nothing comes close to it in the air-to-air role,"
Before bombers or strike aircraft can reach hardened nuclear or missile targets, someone has to clear the skies. That is the F-22's core mission, former generals explained in the webinar.

segments.heading: Effect on U.S. decision-making and options
segments.text: U.S. military officials and former commanders described the deployment as expanding presidential options and serving as a deterrent signal rather than a definitive step toward immediate military action.

citations.quote: "There is a lot of combat capability that's been moved into the region  the more capability, the more assets we put, the more options that the President has. We don't box him in,"
Retired Lt. Gen. Charles Moore ... said the deployment is about expanding presidential options rather than signaling a predetermined strike.

segments.heading: Iran's potential retaliation options
segments.text: Analysts say Iran retains a range of tools to respond if it perceives an attack as existential. These include launching missiles and drones at U.S. forces and regional partners, and mobilizing allied proxy groups, although the scope and choice of responses would depend on Tehran's assessment of the threat.

citations.quote: Iran is believed to have thousands of missiles and drones within range of US troops based in a number of countries in the Middle East and has threatened to strike them, as well as Israel.
Still, the proxies have vowed to defend the Islamic Republic.

segments.heading: Political and basing considerations
segments.text: Commentators noted that basing high-end U.S. aircraft in Israel can reduce some operational constraints present in other regional locations, and that U.S. political leaders have emphasized diplomacy while not ruling out the use of force.

citations.quote: Unlike some Gulf bases, where operational restrictions can apply, Israel offers fewer political constraints, they explained.
he was not happy with Iran and wants to make a deal with Tehran, but warned that "sometimes you have to" use military force.

story.heading: Trump signals displeasure with Iran talks while raising global tariffs after court ruling
story.summary: President Donald Trump said he was "not happy" with Iran's negotiating stance and has not made a final decision on possible strikes, as indirect nuclear talks continue. Separately, after a Supreme Court ruling limited his tariff authority, the administration announced a hike of global import duties to 15%, prompting domestic and international reactions.

segments.heading: Overview
segments.text: President Donald Trump expressed dissatisfaction with Iran's negotiating posture and said he had not yet decided whether to order strikes. At the same time, following a Supreme Court decision that curtailed aspects of his tariff authority, he announced an increase in a global import levy to 15%. Both developments were reported in U.S. media and have prompted reactions from foreign leaders, business groups and government agencies.

citations.quote: "I'm not happy with the fact that they're not willing to give us what we have to have. So I'm not thrilled with that. We'll see what happens, we're talking later."
Donald Trump raised the global duty on imports into the US to 15% on Saturday...

segments.heading: U.S. statements on Iran negotiations and potential military action
segments.text: Trump told reporters he was displeased with how Iran was negotiating and said he had not made a final decision on whether the United States would strike Iran. He acknowledged that military action carries risks of a wider conflict, and referenced past operations cited during his administration. Separately, reports said Iran's foreign minister expected to prepare a draft counterproposal to the U.S. in the coming days as indirect nuclear talks continue.

citations.quote: The president also told reporters that he had yet to make a final decision on striking Iran...
Iran's foreign minister has said he expects to have a draft counterproposal ready within days after nuclear talks with the US this week, while Trump said he was considering limited military strikes.

segments.heading: Tariff increase and Supreme Court ruling
segments.text: After the Supreme Court ruled that much of his emergency tariff authority was unlawful in a 6-3 decision, the administration said it would raise a global import levy to 15%. The administration characterized the move as taking the tariff to a "fully allowed" level under alternative legal authority, while reporting on the timing of the change noted uncertainty over whether official documents had been signed to enact it immediately.

citations.quote: the court's 6-3 ruling that rejected the president's authority to impose tariffs under a 1977 economic emergency powers act
the administration was hiking the import levies 'to the fully allowed, and legally tested, 15% level'.
While Trump's announcement claimed the new tariffs would take effect 'immediately', it was unclear whether any official documents had been signed confirming the timing.

segments.heading: Reactions and immediate implications
segments.text: Business groups and foreign leaders responded to the tariff announcement and the court ruling. Industry associations asked for refunds of previously collected levies covered by the ruling, and the U.S. Chamber of Commerce called for the return of an estimated $133 billion. European leaders signalled concern and a desire for coordinated responses. Separately, the State Department took precautionary steps related to regional tensions by authorizing non-essential U.S. Embassy employees in Jerusalem to leave.

citations.quote: Top associations of American businesses are demanding to be repaid for Trump's tariffs... The US Chamber of Commerce, too, called for swift return of an estimated $133bn in collected tariffs covered by the ruling.
German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, said he would travel to Washington with a coordinated European position, and warned of the 'poison' of more uncertainty.
the State Department appeared to be acting out of caution on Friday as it authorized all non-essential employees at the U.S. Embassy in Jerusalem to leave Israel.

story.heading: Federal indictments in Minnesota church disruption; 5-year-old detained by ICE is released
story.summary: Federal authorities announced additional indictments and arrests related to a January takeover-style disruption at Cities Church in Minnesota, while a 5-year-old boy taken in a separate immigration enforcement action was ordered released by a federal judge and has returned to Minnesota.

segments.heading: Arrests and indictments in Cities Church disruption
segments.text: The Department of Justice unsealed an indictment in late January charging people in connection with a January 18 disruption at Cities Church in Minnesota. Attorney General Pam Bondi said the unsealed indictment charged 30 additional people and that 25 had been arrested at her direction. FBI officials said the arrests demonstrate the bureau will not tolerate attacks on worshippers; federal authorities have, in total, indicted multiple people in the incident.

citations.quote: Today, [the Justice Department] unsealed an indictment charging 30 more people who took part in the attack on Cities Church in Minnesota," Bondi wrote on X. "At my direction, federal agents have already arrested 25 of them, with more to come throughout the day.
"To date, 39 individuals have been indicted over their role in the January 18th targeting on Cities Church  with multiple arrests already occurring last month including Don Lemon.

segments.heading: Allegations in the indictment and reported organization of the disruption
segments.text: The unsealed indictment alleges the group engaged in a coordinated, takeover-style attack that involved intimidation and physical obstruction, and that some individuals organized the disruption under the name "Operation Pullup" and promoted it on social media. The indictment states the defendants conspired to oppress, threaten and intimidate clergy, staff and congregants, and that the disruption forced the termination of the church's worship service.

citations.quote: in a coordinated takeover-style attack and engaged in acts of oppression, intimidation, threats, interference, and physical obstruction.

The indictment states that Armstrong, Allen, Strong and Cullars-Doty, along with others, "organized" the disruption, which they dubbed "Operation Pullup." They allegedly promoted it through Instagram and Facebook.

segments.heading: Official statements and law enforcement framing
segments.text: Officials framed the arrests as enforcement of federal protections for houses of worship and of the right to worship without intimidation. Attorney General Pam Bondi and FBI leadership issued statements saying the Justice Department and FBI would pursue those who target worshippers.

citations.quote: "YOU CANNOT ATTACK A HOUSE OF WORSHIP. If you do so, you cannot hide from us  we will find you, arrest you, and prosecute you. This Department of Justice STANDS for Christians and all Americans of faith,"

"Todays FACE Act arrests with our federal partners show this FBI will never tolerate those who target, attack, or intimidate Americans peacefully exercising their right to worship freely," Patel said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

segments.heading: Detention and release of a 5-year-old taken during an ICE arrest
segments.text: Separately, a 5-year-old boy who was taken by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents in Minnesota on Jan. 20 was held for over a week in Texas with his father before a federal judge ordered their release. The boy, Liam Conejo Ramos, returned to Minnesota after the release. Local authorities, elected officials and the judge criticized the manner of the arrest; ICE disputed some of the characterizations of the event.

citations.quote: Liam Conejo Ramos, the 5-year-old boy who became one of the symbols of the Trump administration's immigration raids in Minnesota, has been released from a Texas detention facility, where he was held for over a week with his father.
were taken by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents while in their driveway after the boy came home from school on Jan. 20.

Agents at the time refused an offer made by another adult living in the home to take the child...the agents led the boy to his front door and ordered him to knock, "essentially using a 5-year-old as bait," according to a news release from Columbia Heights Public Schools.
The pair's release was ordered by U.S. District Judge Fred Biery on Jan. 31.

"The case has its genesis in the ill-conceived and incompetently-implemented government pursuit of daily deportation quotas, apparently even if it requires traumatizing children," Biery wrote in his order.

segments.heading: Differing characterizations and public response
segments.text: The sources present differing emphases for the two events. Coverage of the church disruption centers on criminal allegations and federal enforcement actions and includes forceful statements from DOJ and the FBI. Coverage of the ICE detention highlights the circumstances of the child's removal, public reaction, and a judge's critical ruling; the reporting notes that ICE disputed some claims about the arrest. Both items have prompted public attention and, according to the reporting, protests and commentary.

citations.quote: "YOU CANNOT ATTACK A HOUSE OF WORSHIP... we will find you, arrest you, and prosecute you. This Department of Justice STANDS for Christians and all Americans of faith,"

Photos of the arrest, which showed Ramos outside the home in a character hat with a Spiderman backpack, circulated widely online. The arrest...sparked intense protests in Minneapolis and across the country.

story.heading: Xu Mengtao wins women's aerials in Livigno; New York Times publishes Olympics-themed Connections puzzle answers
story.summary: China's Xu Mengtao won the women's aerials at Livigno, where the final round's single-jump format decided medals. Separately, The New York Times published an Olympics-themed edition of its Connections: Sports Edition puzzle with answers and hints tied to the 2026 Winter Games.

segments.heading: Women's aerials at Livigno: final-round format and results
segments.text: In the Livigno aerials competition, the final six skiers competed under a single-jump medal round in which earlier scores were not carried over; that format produced a range of outcomes when several athletes attempted the same high-difficulty triple. Chinas Xu Mengtao won the event, with Danielle Scott of Australia taking silver and Chinas Shao Qi bronze. Two American finalists fell on their final landings despite having higher earlier scores that would have been medal-worthy if repeated in the final.

citations.quote: Once competitors reach the final six, their previous scores dont matter.
Chinas Xu Mengtao won gold with a 112.90 (up from 107.75), while Australias Danielle Scott (102.17) took silver, and Chinas Shao Qi (101.90) the bronze.

segments.heading: Performance details and athlete perspectives
segments.text: Athletes and observers described aerials as both spectacular and unforgiving, noting that height and mid-air form are scored alongside landings. Two Americans, Kaila Kuhn and Winter Vinecki, had higher scores in earlier rounds but fell on their final landings and finished fifth and sixth respectively; competitors reflected on the difficulty of the Olympic format and on a trend toward higher-difficulty triple maneuvers.

citations.quote: Think of it like gymnastics on skies.
"Its a difficult format, for sure," Kuhn said. "Ive had better runs than that one, and I'm bummed I didn't put a better one down in the medal round."

segments.heading: Connections: Sports Edition publishes Olympics-themed puzzle answers
segments.text: The New York Times' Connections: Sports Edition ran an Olympics-themed puzzle tied to the closing phase of the 2026 Winter Games and published hints and answers for the Feb. 22, 2026 puzzle, including groups that referenced future Olympic hosts and U.S. women's hockey players.

citations.quote: Need help with todays Connections: Sports Edition puzzle? Youve come to the right place.
Future Olympic hosts: BRISBANE, FRENCH ALPS, LOS ANGELES, UTAH

segments.heading: Puzzle format and published answers
segments.text: The Connections: Sports Edition puzzle groups 16 words into four related categories. For the Feb. 22 puzzle the published difficulty rating was 3 out of 5; the article included one reveal for each category and the full answers for players seeking hints or confirmation.

citations.quote: Game No. 517s difficulty: 3 out of 5
Connections: Sports Edition answers for Feb. 22, 2026

story.heading: Milano Cortina: Feb. 18 schedule, Stolz’s bid and key results from Day 15
story.summary: The 2026 Winter Olympics continued with scheduled events and playoff rounds on Feb. 18. U.S. speed skater Jordan Stolz entered with multiple medals and remaining races, men's hockey advanced into knockout rounds, and Day 15 produced standout results including Johannes Høsflot Klæbo's record haul and Canada's curling gold.

segments.heading: Schedule and overall context
segments.text: The Milano Cortina Winter Olympics feature competitors from more than 90 countries across 116 events over 16 days. Competition on Wednesday, Feb. 18 included scheduled speed skating races and the start of the men's hockey quarterfinal round, with broadcast coverage across NBC networks and Peacock.

citations.quote: Athletes from more than 90 countries will compete for Winter Olympic medals in 116 events over 16 days
Here is the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics competition schedule for Wednesday, Feb. 18.

segments.heading: Jordan Stolz: pre-race expectations and reported outcomes
segments.text: U.S. speed skater Jordan Stolz entered the latter stages of the Games as a leading figure after setting Olympic records and winning events. Pre-race coverage described him as a contender to win additional gold medals and to equal a long-standing U.S. speedskating mark; subsequent reporting summarized his final medal total and finish in the mass start.

citations.quote: setting Olympic records en route to winning two gold medals. But he's not done yet, with two more gold medals and a feat that hasn't been done in more than 45 years still within reach
two golds and a silver, I9m pretty happy with that.

segments.heading: Men's hockey: playoff progression and notable outcomes
segments.text: The men's hockey tournament moved into knockout play in Milan. Canada earned the top overall seed after a tiebreaker on goal differential. The United States played in the quarterfinal/semifinal rounds during this phase of the schedule, and the tournament included a bronze-medal game won by Finland in the late stages of the event.

citations.quote: Canada received the top overall seed thanks to winning a tiebreaker against the United States for a better goal differential.
Finland put the disappointment of Friday9s narrow loss to Canada in the rearview mirror and blitzed Slovakia 6-1 to claim the bronze medal in men9s hockey.

segments.heading: Day 15 highlights: Klæbo's record and Canada's curling gold
segments.text: Day 15 featured multiple headline performances. Norway's Johannes Høsflot Klæbo won his sixth gold medal at these Games, a total described as the most golds won by an individual at a single Winter Olympics. In curling, Brad Jacobs and Canada defeated Great Britain to take the men's title.

citations.quote: claiming his sixth gold medal to complete the most dominant individual performance in Winter Olympics history.
he took out Great Britain 9-6 in a tense and strategic battle to win his second Olympic gold medal

story.heading: United States Defeats Sweden in Overtime to Reach Olympic Men's Hockey Semifinals
story.summary: The United States beat Sweden in an overtime quarterfinal at the 2026 Winter Olympics, advancing to face Slovakia in the semifinals. The U.S. victory was decided by Quinn Hughes and supported by a 28-save performance from Connor Hellebuyck. Other quarterfinals advanced Canada and Finland.

segments.heading: Quarterfinal outcome and decisive moments
segments.text: The United States beat Sweden in overtime to win its quarterfinal and advance to the Olympic semifinals. Quinn Hughes scored the overtime goal and had an assist, and goaltender Connor Hellebuyck made key saves in the game, including a 28-save performance overall. Sweden tied the game late in regulation before Hughes ended it in extra time.

citations.quote: Four games. Three went to overtime, including the USA beating Sweden.
The United States' game went a different way. It yielded a late tying goal but outshot the Swedes 6-0 and prevailed on a Quinn Hughes overtime goal.
Connor Hellebuyck made 28 saves for the USA, allowing only Mika Zibanejad's tying goal with 1:31 left in the third period.

segments.heading: Tournament seeding and historical context
segments.text: The United States entered the knockout stage as a top seed after finishing the preliminary round unbeaten, while Sweden arrived with prior Olympic wins over the U.S. in the NHL era. Observers noted that the Americans' preliminary wins came against teams with fewer NHL players, making Sweden a tougher test.

citations.quote: Sweden is 2-0 against the USA in the NHL era of the Winter Olympics.
The Americans finished 3-0 in the preliminary round and earned a bye to the quarterfinals.

segments.heading: Semifinal matchups and schedule
segments.text: With the win over Sweden, the U.S. advanced to play Slovakia in the semifinals. Canada and Finland also advanced and will meet in the other semifinal. The quarterfinal round included multiple overtime games across the bracket.

citations.quote: The USA will face Slovakia, which beat Germany 6-2 in the first game of the day. Canada and Finland will be the other semifinal.
Slovakia will take on Germany in the day's first quarterfinal game, followed by Canada vs. Czechia, Finland vs. Switzerland and the United States vs. Sweden.

segments.heading: Injuries and player availability
segments.text: The quarterfinals included notable player injuries that affected team lineups. During the Canada game, captain Sidney Crosby was checked and left the ice, later going to the dressing room; his status was under review in subsequent coverage. Canada was also noted to be without defenseman Josh Morrissey for later games.

citations.quote: Team Canada captain Sidney Crosby skated slowly toward the dressing room after a heavy check during the second period.
Canada will again be without defenseman Josh Morrissey. Captain Sidney Crosby, injured in the quarterfinals, hadnt been ruled out as of Saturday afternoon.

segments.heading: Broadcasting and concurrent Olympic scheduling

story.heading: NCAA tournament picture firms as Duke's Cameron Boozer stars in win over No. 1 Michigan
story.summary: With conference tournaments approaching, bracket projections are narrowing while Duke freshman Cameron Boozer produced a standout performance in a 68-63 win over top-ranked Michigan, underscoring his status as a leading player this season.

segments.heading: Current NCAA tournament picture and bubble
segments.text: As mid-February approaches the conference tournaments, bracket projections are becoming clearer. Recent projections listed teams on the edge of the field and identified those likely outside it, and described which conferences are emerging as the dominant groups shaping the tournament landscape.

citations.quote: At this point of the 2025-26 season, we have a good idea of which teams are headed to the Big Dance

Georgia, UCLA, Ohio State, TCU are the last four teams in the tourney, through Tuesday, Feb. 17. Conversely, New Mexico, California, Missouri and Santa Clara are the first four teams out of the tournament.

segments.heading: Cameron Boozer's performance in Duke's win over Michigan
segments.text: In a late-February matchup at Capital One Arena, Duke defeated No. 1 Michigan 68-63. Duke freshman Cameron Boozer was the statistical leader for his team and was credited by his coach for a comprehensive impact on the game. Observers noted Boozer's contributions across scoring, rebounding and playmaking during the contest.

citations.quote: the Blue Devils came away with a 68-63 victory.
Boozer led Duke in points (18), rebounds (10) and assists (seven) against the top-ranked team in the sport

segments.heading: Implications and outlook
segments.text: The combination of a tightening tournament bubble and high-profile individual performances shapes expectations heading into conference tournaments. Coverage noted a shift in conference strength this season and highlighted Boozer's season-long impact and award recognition as factors that could influence tournament narratives and team evaluations.

citations.quote: the Big Ten emerging as the conference to watch.

story.heading: Supreme Court Bars Trump Tariffs; White House Governors Dispute, Super Bowl LX Preview and Daily Horoscopes
story.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that the president lacked authority to impose emergency tariffs, a decision that prompted a strong reaction from the White House. Separately, a dispute over invitations to a governors' breakfast preceded a largely attended National Governors Association dinner. Sports and lifestyle notes: Super Bowl LX will feature the New England Patriots and Seattle Seahawks with Bad Bunny scheduled for the halftime show, and USA Today published daily horoscopes for Feb. 18, 2026.

segments.heading: Supreme Court rules Trumps emergency tariffs unlawful
segments.text: The Supreme Court held that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act does not authorize the president to impose tariffs, rejecting a central element of the administration's trade policy. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote that the Court was exercising its limited constitutional role in reaching the decision. The ruling noted it would not address other questions such as refunds. The decision drew an immediate and strong public reaction from the president.

citations.quote: we hold that (the International Emergency Economic Powers Act) does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.
the scene at the White House later in the day as Trump denounced the justices in vitriolic, highly personal terms.

segments.heading: Dispute over governors' invitations preceded NGA dinner
segments.text: President Trump spoke at a National Governors Association dinner and praised state leaders. The event followed a dispute in which the White House initially invited only Republican governors to a working breakfast, prompting pushback from the NGA chair and some Democratic governors, and leading to last-minute invitations to all governors.

citations.quote: �2Working with your states wherever we can, we can achieve so much more  your governors, your great people, your great leaders, Trump said in a speech at the black-tie event.
Trumps administration initially invited only Republicans, leading Stitt to tell governors that the NGA wouldnt play facilitate the Friday morning event.

story.heading: U.S. Border Official Accused of Anti‑Jewish Remark; Ambassador's Expansionist Comment Draws Regional Rebuke
story.summary: Two separate incidents involving senior U.S. officials prompted criticism: a Border Patrol commander was accused of making derogatory comments about an Orthodox U.S. attorney, and the U.S. ambassador to Israel said it "would be fine" if Israel took control of large swathes of territory, prompting a joint condemnation by Middle Eastern states.

segments.heading: Allegations against Border Patrol commander Gregory Bovino
segments.text: Border Patrol commander Gregory Bovino was reported to have made offensive remarks about a Jewish U.S. attorney during a Jan. 12 call, asking whether "Orthodox criminals" take Saturdays off and using the phrase "chosen people" in a belittling way, according to news reports. The comments were reported amid increased scrutiny of federal immigration operations in Minneapolis following two fatal shootings involving federal agents and clashes between officers and protesters.

citations.quote: Bovino reportedly made the remarks after learning that Minnesota U.S. Attorney Daniel Rosen, an Orthodox Jew, could not attend the call because he was observing the Sabbath... Bovino reportedly was frustrated by Rosen's absence, allegedly asking if "Orthodox criminals" take Saturdays off and using the phrase "chosen people" in a belittling way.

Bovino's alleged statements come as the federal official has faced increasing pressure and criticism... Five days before the call, ICE agent Jonathan Ross fatally shot 37-year-old mother of three Renee Nicole Good. A little over two weeks later, federal agents shot and killed ICU nurse Alex Pretti.

segments.heading: U.S. ambassador to Israel's comments and regional condemnation
segments.text: In an interview, U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee said "it would be fine if they took it all" when asked whether Israel has a right to land described in a biblical verse, comments that prompted a joint denunciation by nearly every Middle Eastern country aside from Israel. A statement led by the United Arab Emirates and backed by several other Arab and Muslim-majority states called the remarks "dangerous and inflammatory" and said they violated international law.

citations.quote: "It would be fine if they took it all," Huckabee responded, before adding: "I don't think that's what we're talking about here today."

A statement led by the United Arab Emirates and backed by Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Jordan, Qatar and others denounced Huckabee's comments as "dangerous and inflammatory..." and said they were a "flagrant violation of international law."

segments.heading: Administration actions, prior positions and contrasting statements
segments.text: Reports about Bovino's role and remarks came as the White House reassigned or deployed other officials to Minneapolis; the administration said it sent "border czar Tom Homan" as a point person for immigration enforcement operations. The Department of Homeland Security also issued a denial that Bovino had been relieved of duties while other outlets were reported to have said he would return to a previous posting. On the Middle East issue, the remarks by the ambassador contrasted with earlier statements attributed to President Trump denying he would allow Israeli annexation of the West Bank and with Huckabee's prior expressed support for recognizing Israeli sovereignty.

citations.quote: The White House announced that border czar Tom Homan was being sent to the state as the Trump administration’s new point person for immigration enforcement operations.

Reuters reported that Bovino has been removed from his role as Border Patrol "commander at large" and will return to his former job... Still, Department of Homeland Security spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin denied reports that Bovino was being removed from his assignment, saying... "Chief Gregory Bovino has NOT been relieved of his duties."
But last September Trump said: "I will not allow Israel to annex the West Bank. Nope, I will not allow it. It's not going to happen."

In November 2024... Huckabee made clear that he supports Israel's annexation of the occupied West Bank. He told Israel's Army Radio: "I won't make the policy, I will carry out the policy of the president. But (Trump) has already demonstrated in his first term that there's never been an American president that has been more helpful in securing an understanding of the sovereignty of Israel."

story.heading: Pakistan Says It Carried Out Airstrikes on Militant Camps in Afghanistan; Kabul Reports Civilian Casualties
story.summary: Pakistan announced cross-border airstrikes on militant camps in eastern Afghanistan, saying the operations targeted TTP and Islamic State affiliates. Afghan authorities called the strikes a violation of sovereignty and reported civilian deaths, including women and children. The strikes come after a series of deadly attacks in Pakistan and amid a fragile ceasefire between the neighbours.

segments.heading: Overview of the strikes
segments.text: Pakistan's government said its military carried out airstrikes on militant camps across the border in Afghanistan. Afghan officials confirmed strikes inside Afghanistan and reported civilian harm. Both Islamabad and Kabul have framed the events within a broader pattern of recent cross-border tensions.

citations.quote: Pakistan said Sunday it carried out airstrikes on militant camps across the border in Afghanistan
Pakistan launched multiple airstrikes on Saturday night targeting militants in neighbouring Afghanistan

segments.heading: Targets named and Pakistan's stated justification
segments.text: Pakistan's information ministry and officials said the strikes were "intelligence-based, selective operations" against seven camps belonging to the Pakistani Taliban (TTP) and affiliates, and also targeted an Islamic State affiliate. Islamabad linked the operations to a recent series of deadly attacks on Pakistani soil and said it had evidence tying those attacks to Afghanistan-based militant leadership.

citations.quote: intelligence-based, selective operations

the military conducted 'intelligence-based, selective operations' against seven camps belonging to the Pakistani Taliban, known as Tehreek-e-Taliban Pakistan (TTP), and its affiliates. He said an affiliate of Islamic State was also targeted in the border region.

segments.heading: Afghan response and reported civilian casualties
segments.text: Afghanistan's Ministry of Defense described the strikes as a violation of its sovereignty and said they occurred in civilian areas of Nangarhar and Paktika provinces. Afghan officials and state media reported civilian casualties, including women and children; local officials provided specific casualty figures that differ from other accounts.

citations.quote: a 'blatant violation of Afghanistan's national sovereignty' and a 'clear breach' of international law
the strikes targeted civilians in Nangarhar and Paktika provinces, killing and wounding dozens of people, including women and children.
Women and children were among the 18 people killed, Sayed Tayeb Hamad, a senior police official in Nangarhar, said

segments.heading: Recent attacks in Pakistan cited as motive
segments.text: Pakistani officials linked the strikes to a month of deadly attacks inside Pakistan, including a suicide blast at a Shia mosque in Islamabad and attacks on security forces. Islamabad said it had "conclusive evidence" that militants carried out recent assaults at the direction of Afghanistan-based leadership and handlers.

citations.quote: conclusive evidence... that the February attacks were carried out by militants at the 'behest of their Afghanistan based leadership and handlers.'
the blast... killing 11 soldiers and a child.

segments.heading: Ceasefire, diplomacy and potential implications
segments.text: Analysts and officials framed the strikes against the backdrop of a fragile ceasefire and prior cross-border clashes since October. Pakistan and Afghanistan have engaged in mediated talks previously; Pakistan urged the international community to press Afghanistan's authorities to prevent militant use of Afghan soil. Observers note the strikes will test existing agreements and regional stability.

citations.quote: This new escalation will test the delicate ceasefire that has been in place between the neighboring countries since last October
A Qatar-mediated ceasefire has largely held, but talks in Istanbul failed to produce a formal agreement, and relations remain strained.

Pakistan said Sunday it carried out airstrikes on militant camps across the border in Afghanistan, in a serious test of an uneasy peace between the neighbors.

Pakistan’s information mini…

story.heading: Nine killed and six rescued after avalanche in California's Sierra Nevada
story.summary: An avalanche struck a guided backcountry skiing group near Lake Tahoe, killing nine people including three professional guides and injuring or burying others. Recovery operations concluded after several days amid severe winter conditions; authorities have opened an investigation.

segments.heading: What happened
segments.text: A large avalanche struck a backcountry skiing group near Castle Peak in the Sierra Nevada on Tuesday during severe winter weather. The slide overtook members of a multi-day guided trip as they were returning from huts and cabins in the Frog Lake area. Emergency responders were alerted by an emergency beacon and radio dispatches reported multiple people buried.

citations.quote: Medical for avalanche in the area of Castle Peak,
“Avalanche!” one of them yelled.

segments.heading: Casualties and identities
segments.text: Officials say nine people were killed and six survivors were rescued. Among the dead were three professional guides and six members of the traveling group. Families of those killed described them as mothers, friends and experienced backcountry skiers.

citations.quote: Six of the close friends and three guides are among the nine people killed in the avalanche
They were identified as Carrie Atkin, Liz Clabaugh, Danielle Keatley, Kate Morse, Caroline Sekar and Kate Vitt

segments.heading: Rescue and recovery efforts
segments.text: Search and rescue operations involved multiple agencies and volunteers over several days. Weather and snow conditions delayed body recoveries; officials completed the operation with air support on the final day and credited local volunteers and responding agencies for their roles.

citations.quote: While we wish we could have saved them all, we are grateful that we can bring them home,
were able to finish the rescue operations with the assistance of a Black Hawk helicopter in the snowy region

segments.heading: Weather and avalanche conditions
segments.text: Forecasters and survivors described a major winter storm and fresh unstable snow at the time of the slide. Officials and experts characterized the avalanche as large; some reports compared the slide path to the size of a football field and noted heavy snowfall that day.

citations.quote: the blizzard arrived, delivering blankets of unstable powder
The avalanche has been the deadliest since 1981

segments.heading: Investigation and local response
segments.text: Authorities said they are investigating the incident, including whether criminal negligence contributed. Local forest officials temporarily closed the terrain; families of the victims issued statements expressing grief and unanswered questions.

citations.quote: The sheriff's office said it is investigating whether criminal negligence contributed to the incident.
We are devastated beyond words,

The avalanche…

story.heading: Trump announces increase of global tariffs to 15% after Supreme Court ruling
story.summary: President Donald Trump said he would raise a temporary global tariff to 15% after the US Supreme Court ruled his earlier use of a 10% tariff under the IEEPA exceeded his authority. The move shifts enforcement to a different trade law with limits and timeframes and has drawn reactions from foreign governments, businesses and economists amid questions about timing and exemptions.

segments.heading: Announcement of a 15% global tariff
segments.text: President Trump announced via social media that he would raise a previously announced 10% worldwide tariff to 15%, saying the increase was ‘‘effective immediately.’’ He said his administration would determine and issue the details of the new tariffs over the coming months.

citations.quote: effective immediately, he would be "raising the 10% Worldwide Tariff on Countries  to the fully allowed, and legally tested, 15% level."

I, as President of the United States of America, will be, effective immediately, raising the 10% Worldwide Tariff on Countries, many of which have been 'ripping' the U.S. off for decades, without retribution (until I came along!), to the fully allowed, and legally tested, 15% level.

segments.heading: Supreme Court ruling and legal context
segments.text: The Supreme Court ruled that the way the administration had imposed the earlier 10% tariff exceeded presidential authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The court decision prompted the administration to shift to a different legal authority for a temporary tariff.

citations.quote: The court on Friday ruled that Trump exceeded his authority with the way he imposed tariffs on trading partners using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA)
the US supreme court ruled against the legality of his flagship trade policy

segments.heading: Authority, duration and other trade tools
segments.text: The administration said it would rely on Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 to implement the temporary 15% levy; that authority allows a president to impose tariffs of up to 15% for up to 150 days, after which congressional approval is required. The administration has other trade tools it could use for targeted duties and has previously used Section 232 and Section 301 authorities.

citations.quote: The law according to section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974  allows the president to impose a levy of up to 15% for 150 days
Presidents can impose up to 15% in tariffs using Section 122, but those duties are temporary and require congressional approval after 150 days.

Trump may continue imposing duties using other trade laws. The administration has already used Section 232 investigations to impose across-the-board duties on steel, aluminum, copper, lumber, furniture, cars and car parts.

segments.heading: Reactions and potential economic effects
segments.text: Governments and business groups expressed concern and said they would assess implications. Analysts and trade groups noted the 15% levy would change the effective tariff burden for different countries and industries, and that many of the costs to date had been borne by US businesses and consumers. Some countries and companies could face lower or higher rates compared with prior IEEPA-based levies, depending on prior reciprocal tariffs.

citations.quote: Australia will "examine all options" after the US president Donald Trump announced a temporary 15% tariff would apply to US imports from all countries.

The 15% duties would make this year
nd#8217;s effective tariff rate 6%, according to Erica York, vice president of federal tax policy at the Tax Foundation.
German chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Saturday that he would travel to Washington with a coordinated European position  and warned of the "poison" of more uncertainty.

segments.heading: Timing, exemptions and outstanding questions
segments.text: Officials and reports said timing and implementation details were unclear. A White House fact sheet had set an earlier 10% levy to take effect on Tuesday, but it was not clear whether documents to enact the 15% rate had been signed or whether the White House would confirm an effective date. Reports also said certain products would be exempted from the temporary tariffs.

citations.quote:
The White House did not respond to CNN
nd#8217;s request for comment on whether the 15% tariff will take effect on Tuesday.

While Trump
nd#8217;s announcement claimed that the new tariffs would take effect "immediately," it was unclear whether any official documents had been signed confirming the timing.
Some products will be exempted from the temporary tariffs including critical minerals, metals and pharmaceuticals.

articles.title: Trump announces global tariffs will increase to 15% from 10% ‘effective immediately’

The court on Friday ruled t…

The US president’s move came less than 24 hours…

story.heading: Ukraine Condemns Hungary and Slovakia Over Threats to Cut Electricity Amid Pipeline Dispute
story.summary: Ukraine accused Hungary and Slovakia of issuing "ultimatums and blackmail" after the two countries threatened to stop emergency electricity supplies unless Kyiv allows the resumption of Russian oil transit through its territory. The dispute follows a January pipeline outage and occurs alongside other wartime security incidents in Ukraine.

segments.heading: Standoff over energy supplies and mutual accusations
segments.text: Ukraine's Foreign Ministry described threats from Hungary and Slovakia to stop emergency electricity supplies as "ultimatums and blackmail." Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico and Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán have both linked continued electricity flows to the resumption of Russian oil transit through Ukraine. Kyiv rejected the demands and said such pressure should be directed at Moscow rather than Ukraine.

citations.quote: Ukraine rejects and condemns the ultimatums and blackmail by the governments of Hungary and the Slovak Republic regarding energy supplies between our countries," the Ukrainian Foreign Ministry said in a statement. "Ultimatums should be sent to the Kremlin, and certainly not to Kyiv.
If oil supplies to Slovakia are not resumed on Monday, I will ask SEPS, the state-owned joint-stock company, to stop emergency electricity supplies to Ukraine," Fico said in a post on X.

segments.heading: Pipeline outage and reliance on Druzhba route
segments.text: Shipments of Russian oil to Hungary and Slovakia were cut off after a January 27 incident that Kyiv says involved a Russian drone strike damaging pipeline equipment in western Ukraine. Slovakia and Hungary are among the few EU countries that continue to receive substantial Russian oil via the Soviet-era Druzhba pipeline traversing Ukraine.

citations.quote: Shipments of Russian oil to Hungary and Slovakia have been cut off since January 27, when Kyiv says a Russian drone strike hit pipeline equipment in Western Ukraine.
Slovakia and Hungary are the only two EU countries that still rely on significant amounts of Russian oil shipped via the Soviet-era Druzhba pipeline over Ukraine.

segments.heading: Effects on Ukraine's electricity supplies and Kyiv's proposals
segments.text: Ukraine says it relies increasingly on emergency electricity imports from neighbouring EU members after Russian attacks damaged its grid. Kyiv and its mission to the EU have proposed alternative routes for oil transit while pipeline repairs proceed, including using Ukraine's oil transportation system and maritime options such as the Odesa-Brody link.

citations.quote: Between them, Hungary and Slovakia have been providing around half of European emergency electricity exports to Ukraine, which Kyiv increasingly relies on as Russian attacks have damaged its grid.
Ukraine has proposed alternative transit routes to ship oil to Europe while emergency pipeline repair works are under way.

segments.heading: Wider political and security context
segments.text: The dispute between Kyiv and the two EU and NATO members occurs amid other wartime developments. Hungary has also signalled broader leverage, including a threat reported to block a proposed €90bn Ukrainian war loan. Separately, security incidents such as explosions in Kyiv and an attack in Lviv that wounded civilians and killed a police officer were reported by Ukrainian authorities.

citations.quote: Hungary has also threatened to block a €90bn Ukrainian war loan.
Powerful explosions shook Ukraine's capital Kyiv early on Sunday, after officials warned of a possible ballistic missile attack and ordered residents into shelters.

story.heading: Salsa legend Willie Colón dies at 75
story.summary: Willie Colón, a pioneering trombonist, vocalist and composer who helped shape salsa music, has died at age 75, his family said. Colón recorded dozens of albums, sold millions of records and was recognized with multiple industry honors over a career spanning decades.

segments.heading: Death and family statement
segments.text: Willie Colón died on Saturday at age 75, his family announced. The family asked for privacy while noting they were grateful for support and remembered his music and the memories he created. The cause of death was not disclosed.

citations.quote: It is with profound sadness that we announce the passing of our beloved husband, father, and renowned musician, Willie Colón. He passed away peacefully this morning surrounded by his beloved family.
While we grieve his absence, we also rejoice in the timeless gift of his music and the cherished memories he created that will live on forever,

segments.heading: Career overview and musical style
segments.text: Born in the Bronx to Puerto Rican parents, Colón signed with Fania Records as a teenager and developed a style that blended elements of jazz, rock and salsa with rhythms from Cuba, Puerto Rico, Brazil and Africa. His work often explored Puerto Rican identity and the experience of living in the United States.

citations.quote: Colón’s music combined elements of jazz, rock and salsa, incorporating the rhythms of traditional music from Cuba, Puerto Rico, Brazil, and Africa, the biography said.

A significant overarching theme in Colón’s music—which draws from many cultures and several different styles—is an exploration of the competing associations that Puerto Ricans have with their home and with the United States,
Willie didn’t just change salsa. He expanded it, politicized it, clothed it in urban chronicles and took it to stages where it hadn’t been before. His trombone was the voice of the people.

segments.heading: Notable works and recognitions
segments.text: Colón released dozens of albums during his career. He signed with Fania at 15 and released his first album, El Malo, in 1967. His collaborations include the 1978 album Siembra, recorded with Rubén Blades, which includes the hit 'Pedro Navaja' and is described as the biggest-selling salsa album of all time. Over his career he sold millions of records and received industry honors, including a 2004 lifetime achievement award from the Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences.

citations.quote: He signed with Fania at age 15 and two years later, in 1967, released his first album El Malo, which has sold more than 300,000 copies, according to his biography on the LA Philharmonic website.
With more than 30m albums sold, multiple platinum records and 11 combined Grammy and Latin Grammy nominations, Colón is among the most successful salsa artists of all time.
His 1978 album Siembra, recorded with Rubén Blades, includes the hit Pedro Navaja and remains the biggest-selling salsa album of all time.
In 2004, he received a lifetime achievement award from the Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences.

segments.heading: Activism, honors and industry reaction
segments.text: Colón was involved in social causes and served on organizations related to Latino health and immigration. Industry statements and colleagues highlighted his influence on Latin music and his role in bringing the genre to wider audiences.

citations.quote: A longtime social activist, Colón was a member of the Latino Commission on AIDS and the United Nations Immigrant Foundation and was a board member at the Congressional Hispanic Caucus Institute, according to the biography.
We are heartbroken by the passing of an icon whose sound transcended the dancefloor and defined an era.
Billboard magazine named the musician as one of the 30 most influential Latin artists of all time in 2015.

story.heading: Artemis II launch outlook uncertain after hydrogen leaks and a helium flow anomaly
story.summary: NASA's Artemis II crewed lunar flyby faces potential delay after pre-launch tests identified super-chilled liquid hydrogen leaks and, later, an interrupted helium flow. The agency is considering rolling the rocket back to the Vehicle Assembly Building, a step that officials say is likely to affect the March launch window. Engineers cite fuel handling trade-offs and an ongoing investigation into the issues.

segments.heading: Launch status and schedule implications
segments.text: NASA announced it is taking steps that could roll the Artemis II rocket and Orion spacecraft back to the Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center after identifying an interrupted helium flow. The agency said that this action "will almost assuredly impact the March launch window." Previously, NASA had been targeting 6 March for the mission but noted there was still preparatory work to be done after earlier testing issues.

citations.quote: Nasa said in a blog post on Saturday it is taking steps to potentially roll back the Artemis II rocket launch after discovering an interrupted flow of helium.
"This will almost assuredly impact the March launch window," Nasa said.

Nasa had said on Friday it was targeting 6 March for the launch of four astronauts around the moon and back as part of its Artemis II mission after successfully completing a fueling test that had caused it to stand down earlier this month.

segments.heading: Hydrogen leaks during wet dress rehearsal
segments.text: During a wet dress rehearsal in early February, launch controllers detected super-chilled liquid hydrogen leaking at the launchpad. The leaks recurred and prompted controllers to halt fuel flow multiple times; the problem prevented completion of the full test and initiated more than a week of investigations and repairs.

citations.quote: Just a few hours into a pre-launch test called a wet dress rehearsal in early February, launch controllers found that enough super-chilled liquid hydrogen fuel was leaking at the launchpad to prompt safety concerns.
The hydrogen leaks kept cropping up, forcing NASA to halt fuel flow to the rocket multiple times.
The issue ultimately left the space agency unable to complete the full test and led to more than a week of investigations and repairs.

segments.heading: Technical trade-offs: why hydrogen is used despite risks
segments.text: Hydrogen is difficult to contain because it is the lightest element and can escape from containment, but its low density gives it a high specific impulse, making it an efficient rocket propellant. Engineers describe this as a performance trade-off: hydrogen's properties increase handling challenges on the ground while providing better fuel efficiency for launch and deep-space missions.

citations.quote: It "tends to find its way out of things you want to try to contain it in," said Adam Swanger, a senior principal investigator and cryogenics research engineer at NASA's Kennedy Space Center.
"The low density is good for performance," Swanger told CNN. "So there's kind of a trade-off there."

segments.heading: Helium flow anomaly and operational response
segments.text: Separately from the hydrogen issues, NASA observed an interrupted flow of helium in the rocket's interim cryogenic propulsion stage. Helium flow is required for launch operations, and the agency said it is taking steps to roll Artemis II and Orion back to the Vehicle Assembly Building to address the anomaly.

citations.quote: The agency observed overnight an interrupted flow of helium in the space launch system rocket's interim cryogenic propulsion stage. Helium flow is required for launch.
The agency said it is taking steps to roll the Artemis II rocket and Orion spacecraft back to the vehicle assembly building at the Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

segments.heading: Mission overview and historical context
segments.text: Artemis II is planned as a crewed, roughly 10-day lunar flyby with four astronauts—three Americans and one Canadian—intended to test systems for future deep-space exploration. The mission will not land but is expected to travel beyond the far side of the moon. NASA's engineers have faced similar hydrogen containment challenges in previous programs, including delays on Artemis I and other launch vehicle tests.

citations.quote: Nasa had said on Friday it was targeting 6 March for the launch of four astronauts around the moon and back as part of its Artemis II mission...
The mission will fly about 4,700 miles (7,600km) beyond the far side of the moon, surpassing the distance record set by Apollo 13 in 1970.

Liftoff of an uncrewed test flight around the moon in 2022, called Artemis I, was delayed several times then nearly thwarted by similar hydrogen seepage before a team of jumpsuit-clad NASA workers swept in at the 11th hour and manually fixed a leaky valve.

For example, the Vulcan Centaur rocket... uses hydrogen to power the upper part of its rocket. And in 2023, a fuel leak caused a fireball explosion during Vulcan Centaur testing in Alabama, damaging nearby infrastructure and delaying the rocket's inaugural launch.
